coredata.dc:title: Tricolor emission carbon dots for label-free ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric recognition of Al3+ and pyrophosphate ion and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Lihong
Wang, Qianliang
Zhang, Caihong
Zhang, Guomei
Zhang, Yan
Dong, Chuan
Shuang, Shaomin
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric determination endows fluorescence (FL) sensors improving accuracy and sensitivity by measuring intensity ratio of well-resolved emission peaks. Herein, tricolor-emission carbon dots (T-CDs) have been developed for ratiometric recognition of Al3+ and pyrophosphate ion (PPi). T-CDs were fabricated from hematoxylin and Tris by one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As-prepared T-CDs engender amusing triple emission bands, including bright yellow and orange FL, but weak blue FL. Upon introducing Al3+, yellow and orange FL is distinctly quenched while blue one is enhanced. On subsequent addition of PPi, T-CDs’ FL can be partially recovered. The ratiometric fluorescent changes of T-CDs have been utilized to detect Al3+ and PPi in aqueous solution with linear ranges of 5–15 μM and 0.75–10 μM, respectively, as well as limits of detection of 0.8 nM and 2.2 nM, respectively. More importantly, laser scanning confocal microscope imaging of HeLa cells demonstrates that T-CDs could be applied to visually monitor Al3+ and PPi fluctuations in living cells, illustrating that proposed T-CDs have tremendous potential in biological systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tricolor emission
Carbon dots
Al3+
PPi
Ratiometric fluorescent
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Al3+ and PPi detection in tap water and human blood serum samples
Determination of Al3+ and PPi in living cells

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21874087 ) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of T-CDs and the applications for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detection of Al3+ and PPi.
(A–C) T-CDs’ TEM images. (D) T-CDs’ size distribution. (E) T-CDs’ AFM image. (Inset: T-CDs’ height). (F) T-CDs’ height distribution.
(A) T-CDs’ XPS full spectrum. (B–D) C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of T-CDs, respectively.
(A) T-CDs’ UV–vis and excitation-emission spectra. (B) FL decay of T-CDs at 570 nm (excitation at 420 nm).

(A) T-CDs’ FL spectra with adding various Al3+ concentrations (0–22.5 μM). (B) The relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and Al3+ concentrations at 0–22.5 μM. (Inset: responsive linearity.) (C) T-CDs’ UV–vis spectra with adding various Al3+ concentrations (0–22.5 μM). (D) Linear relationship between absorbance intensity ratios (A 278/A 540) and Al3+ concentrations at 0–22.5 μM. (E) Color changes of T-CDs solution with various concentrations of Al3+ (0–22.5 μM) under daylight.

(A) FL spectra of T-CDs@Al3+ with adding various PPi concentrations (0–400 μM). (B) The relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and PPi concentrations (0–400 μM). (Inset: responsive linearity.) (C) UV–vis spectra of T-CDs@Al3+ with adding various PPi concentrations (0–300 μM). (D) The relationship between absorbance intensity ratios (A 278/A 540) and PPi concentrations (0–300 μM). (Inset: responsive linearity.) (E) Color changes of T-CDs@Al3+ ([Al3+] = 22.5 μM) with various concentrations of PPi (0–400 μM) under daylight.
(A) Possible binding mode of T-CDs and Al3+. (B) Possible structure of the chelate composed of Al3+ and PPi.

Laser scanning confocal microscope images of HeLa cells with T-CDs (A), T-CDs@Al3+ (B), and T-CDs@Al3++PPi (C) under 405 nm laser excitation. From left to right: channel 1 (λem = 425–525 nm), channel 2 (λem = 525–585 nm), channel 3 (λem = 585–685 nm), overlay of channel 1, channel 2 and channel 3, and ratio. (D) The relationship between intracellular FL intensities and T-CDs, T-CDs@Al3+ and T-CDs@Al3++PPi, respectively. (E) The relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and T-CDs, T-CDs@Al3+ and T-CDs@Al3++PPi, respectively.

head.ce:title: Tricolor emission carbon dots for label-free ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric recognition of Al3+ and pyrophosphate ion and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric determination endows fluorescence (FL) sensors improving accuracy and sensitivity by measuring intensity ratio of well-resolved emission peaks. Herein, tricolor-emission carbon dots (T-CDs) have been developed for ratiometric recognition of Al3+ and pyrophosphate ion (PPi). T-CDs were fabricated from hematoxylin and Tris by one-pot hydrothermal treatment. As-prepared T-CDs engender amusing triple emission bands, including bright yellow and orange FL, but weak blue FL. Upon introducing Al3+, yellow and orange FL is distinctly quenched while blue one is enhanced. On subsequent addition of PPi, T-CDs’ FL can be partially recovered. The ratiometric fluorescent changes of T-CDs have been utilized to detect Al3+ and PPi in aqueous solution with linear ranges of 5–15 μM and 0.75–10 μM, respectively, as well as limits of detection of 0.8 nM and 2.2 nM, respectively. More importantly, laser scanning confocal microscope imaging of HeLa cells demonstrates that T-CDs could be applied to visually monitor Al3+ and PPi fluctuations in living cells, illustrating that proposed T-CDs have tremendous potential in biological systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have grown as superior candidates to traditional quantum dots [1,2] for sensing and bioimaging applications owing to adjustable fluorescence (FL) [3], fabulous photostability [4], excellent aqueous solubility [5], low toxicity [6] and admirable biocompatibility [7]. Recently, on account of environmental issues, green chemistry routes for synthesizing CDs have raised widespread concerns. Hu et al. [8] prepared blue fluorescent CDs from flax straw via one-step hydrothermal means for “on-off-on” sensing Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid. Wei et al. [9] synthesized fluorescent CDs using natural gynostemma as the precursor through calcination method for bioimaging and antioxidant in zebrafish. Huang et al. [10] used sugarcane molasses to fabricate fluorescent CDs. So far, most of CDs presented single emission and were generally designed as single-wavelength intensity-modulated probes [11,12]. A few of single-emission CDs combining with other fluorophores were developed as ratiometric fluorescent probes, which can improve detection reliability and accuracy by the measurement of intensity ratio of well-resolved fluorescent peaks in comparation with intensity-based probes. Gui et al. [13] combined red-emissive CDs with blue-emissive SiDs as ratiometric FL sensor to detect ciprofloxacin. Bu et al. [14] combined blue-emitting CDs with red-emitting copper nanoclusters as dual-emitting ratiometric FL nanoprobes to determine arginine and acetaminophen, respectively.

As a beneficial supplement to single-emission fluorescent CDs, dual-emission CDs and triple-emission CDs (T-CDs) are considered as a favorable choice for chemical and biological sensing because they could be devised as ratiometric fluorescent probes [15,16]. Currently, dual-emission CDs have been developed for ratiometric FL sensing. Jiang et al. [17] developed dual-emission CDs for fluorometric dual-channel ratiometric determination of fungicide cymoxanil based on analyte-induced aggregation of silver nanoparticles. T-CDs have also been reported. Zhang et al. [18] designed T-CDs with multiple-cores@shell structure using aphen and citric acid as raw materials by hydrothermal method, which were used to fabricate white-light-emitting diode with temperature stability and good anti-photobleaching. Peng et al. [19] synthesized T-CDs using citric acid and urea as the raw materials by the combination of hydrothermal treatment and annealing at relatively low temperature. Zhang et al. [20] fabricated T-CDs using 5-amino-1,10-phenanthroline and citric acid as carbon sources via one-pot hydrothermal treatment for ultra-wide range pH test papers and bioimaging. However, these T-CDs haven’t been utilized for ratiometric FL sensing. Therefore, it is of urgent need to design T-CDs with ratiometric FL sensing capability.

In the human body, aluminum is one of crucial trace elements, which plays a pivotal role in disease diagnosis [21]. In the light of WHO recommendations, the upper limit for weekly dietary intake of aluminum for human has been set to be 7 mg kg−1 body weight [22]. Nevertheless, superfluous intake of Al3+ can result in many diseases, such as Parkinson's disease [23], osteoporosis [24], amyotrophic lateral sclerosis [25], Alzheimer's disease [26] and breast cancer [27]. Therefore, the detection of Al3+ levels becomes of extraordinary significance for diagnosing early disease [28]. Some conventional methods of Al3+ detection have been reported such as high-performance liquid chromatography, voltammetry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, and electrochemical method [29,30]. Nevertheless, most of them usually suffer from some shortcomings like the requirement of complex pretreatment, exorbitant instruments and lengthy operations [31,32]. Thus, many researchers try to design chemical sensors to analyze Al3+. Up to now, CDs based Al3+ fluorescent sensors have been developed. Bhamoreet et al. [33] developed blue-emssion CDs from pears for Al3+ detection through chelation enhanced FL mechanism. Rao et al. [34] designed dual-emission CDs by solvothermal process of Cinnamomum plants’ leaves for ratiometric fluorescent recognition of Al3+. Song et al. [35] synthesized dual-emitting CDs by one-step solvothermal method of red tea to detect Al3+. These Al3+ sensors employed single-emission or dual-emission CDs. Nevertheless, T-CDs for discerning Al3+ have not been reported. Thus, it is particularly significant to excavate T-CDs for trace detection of Al3+.

Pyrophosphate ion (P2O7 4−, PPi), a hydrolysis product of adenosine triphosphate [36], plays an extremely vital role in biological process [37], including DNA polymerization [38], cellular metabolism [39], ATP hydrolysis [40] and enzymatic reactions [41]. However, high PPi concentration could lead to bone mineralization defects through the inhibition of hydroxyapatite deposition, namely, hypophosphatasia [42]. Therefore, monitoring PPi content is fairly pivotal for physiological function and clinical diagnosis. Current analytical techniques for the detection of PPi include enzymatic analysis, capillary electrophoresis, mass spectrometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry, and atomic absorption spectroscopy [43,44]. However, these analytical methods involve some drawbacks such as the requirement of expensive and specialized instruments, lengthy measurement, and complex pre-sample pretreatments, which limit their applications in the detection of PPi [36,45]. Fluorometry has attracted a great deal of attention because of its simple operation and rapid response. At present, CDs based PPi fluorescent sensors have been constructed. Hu et al. [46] exploited strong blue FL nitrogen-doped CDs and utilized CDs@Cu2+ system for the detection of PPi based on preferential bind of PPi to Cu2+. Hu et al. [47] designed blue fluorescent CDs by using waste polyethylene terephthalate as carbon source and employed CDs@Fe3+ as fluorescent sensors for “off-on” determination of PPi. Gao et al. [45] devised dual-emitting CDs and proposed CDs@Pb2+ sensing system as ratiometric fluorescent probe to determine PPi. Nevertheless, T-CDs based PPi sensors have been scarcely explored. Hence, it is of exigent demand to develop PPi fluorescent probe based on T-CDs.

Herein, a facile and convenient strategy was utilized to fabricate T-CDs from hematoxylin and Tris via one-pot hydrothermal process. Intriguingly, obtained T-CDs manifest three emission peaks at 473, 570, and 606 nm at 420 nm excitation, which could be employed as sensing platform for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric dual-readout recognition of Al3+ and PPi enduringly in aqueous solution. Coupled with the characteristics of superior biocompatibility and low toxicity, as-prepared T-CDs have been successfully performed for visualizing intracellular Al3+ and PPi fluctuations (Scheme 1 ).

One-pot hydrothermal mean was utilized to manufacture T-CDs. Tris (0.1 g) and hematoxylin (0.35 g) were synchronously placed in 40 mL DDI water. Afterwards, the above system was placed to a 60 mL autoclave for 3 h at 220 °C. Later, the turbid liquid was operated through centrifugating. Then, T-CDs solution was collected after 24 h of dialysis through a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 100–500 Da, which was kept in reserve at 4 °C for further application.

80 μL T-CDs solution (4.3 g L−1) was added to 300 μL Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.2) at 0.9 g L−1 final concentration, and then different concentrations of Al3+ standard were introduced. For the detection of PPi, PPi with different concentrations was introduced to T-CDs@Al3+ solution (0.9 g L−1 T-CDs, 22.5 μM Al3+). After reacting for 40 s under ambient condition, FL spectra were documented.

TEM images (Fig. 1 A–C) present that T-CDs have pseude-spherical appearance and fantastic dispersity. Fig. 1D illustrates that T-CDs’ size distribution falls in the scope of 1.0–5.0 nm and T-CDs’ even diameter is about 3.18 ± 0.82 nm. AFM image of T-CDs (Fig. 1E) depicts superior dispersion of T-CDs. Height range of T-CDs is 3.5–5.5 nm and even height is ca. 4.8 nm (Fig. 1F). The analytical results of AFM are consistent with TEM.

XPS full spectrum of T-CDs (Fig. 2 A) presents three chief bands at 283.5, 397.5, and 530.1 eV, belonging to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. C1s spectrum of T-CDs (Fig. 2B) is decomposed into four peaks, including CC (284.8 eV), CO (286.3 and 286.6 eV) and CO (288.3 eV). High-resolution spectrum of N1s is separated into two peaks at 399.8 and 401.9 eV (Fig. 2C), which could be ascribed to CNC and N(C3). O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) is differentiated as two bands at 531.2 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to the existence of CO and COC/COH, respectively. Additionally, FTIR was carried out to describe functional groups of T-CDs’ surface (Fig. S1). A peak at 3143.69 cm−1represents OH stretching vibration. The peaks located between 2932.11 and 2880.32 cm−1ascribe to CH stretching vibration. Two peaks at 1558.8 and 1401.93 cm−1 show the presence of COO−. The peak at 1456.7 cm−1is attributed to CH bending vibration. The peak at 1045.65 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching vibration. These results of FTIR are coincident with XPS. Based on the above results, prepared T-CDs’ surface is covered with carboxyl groups and negatively charged. Because the same charges repel each other, T-CDs do not aggregate and show good dispersion.

Excitation-emission spectra (Fig. 3 A) express that T-CDs have intriguing triple-emission behavior. Blue emission peak of T-CDs may result from carbon core, which shows excitation-dependent FL behavior. Yellow and orange emission peaks may originate from surface state, presenting excitation-dependence [48]. UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3A) states clearly two absorption bands at 278 and 540 nm, attributable to π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO [12,49]. FL quantum yield of T-CDs was 23.7 % with Rhodamine 6 G as a reference. Average FL lifetime of T-CDs was investigated to be 4.2844 ns (Fig. 3B).

For Al3+ sensing, it is found that upon adding Al3+ FL intensity of blue emission peak increases while that of yellow and orange emission peaks decreases. As shown in Fig. 3A, under 420 nm excitation blue emission peak (473 nm) is weaker and the peak shape is relatively complete while yellow and orange emission peaks (570 and 606 nm) are stronger. To achieve a better ratiometric sensing effect excitation wavelength of 420 nm is chosen.

To appraise T-CDs’ selectivity, the influences of representative metal ions, anions and amino acids on FL intensity were measured, respectively. FL intensity ratio (I 473/I 570) is considerably increased upon adding Al3+ while other competitive substances don’t show the same phenomenon (Fig. S2A–C), implying fantastic selectivity of T-CDs for Al3+. What’s more, other competitive substances with high concentrations (up to 22.5 μM) don’t exhibit remarkable interference (Fig. S2A–C).

T-CDs’ sensing capability for Al3+ was studied in the scope of 0–22.5 μM at pH 7.2 condition under 420 nm excitation. With the increase of Al3+ concentrations, FL intensities of T-CDs at both 570 and 606 nm progressively decline while that at 473 nm gradually enhances (Fig. 4 A). Fig. 4B exhibits the relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and Al3+ concentrations and an excellent linear relationship is fitted in the scope of 5–15 μM with R2 = 0.9891. The limit of detection (LOD) of Al3+ is estimated to be 0.8 nM based on the signal/noise ratio of 3, 9250 times lower than tolerable limit (7.4 μM) of Al3+ in drinking water allowed by WHO [50]. Compared with previous works in Table S1, the proposed Al3+ probe is more sensitive obviously.

The absorbance titrations were implemented to investigate Al3+ sensing capacity of T-CDs. With increasing Al3+ concentrations, the absorbance intensity at 540 nm reduces by degrees accompanying with a ratio enhancement at 278 nm (Fig. 4C). With the increase of Al3+ concentrations from 0 to 22.5 μM, A 278/A 540 linearly decreases from 1.36 to 1.66 (Fig. 4D). The LOD is calculated to be 0.3 μM. As shown in inset of Fig. 4C, upon adding Al3+ to T-CDs solution, Al3+ and O of carboxyl groups on T-CDs’ surface interact, triggering the emergence of a new shoulder peak at 305 nm, the increase of absorption intensity at 278 nm, and the decrease of absorption intensity at 540 nm.

T-CDs’ colorimetric sensing ability was examined through tracking color changes upon introduction of Al3+ under daylight. Al3+ results in an obvious colour variety of T-CDs solution from orange to pink (Fig. 4E), which may result from the strong combination of T-CDs with Al3+. Therefore, we can utilize colorimetric means to discriminate Al3+. Above results display that T-CDs are an outstanding triple-signal Al3+ sensor.

To evaluate the selectivity of T-CDs@Al3+, the influences of representative metal ions, anions and amino acids on FL intensity were measured. FL intensity ratio (I 473/I 570) gradually decreases upon adding PPi while other competitive substances don’t show the same phenomenon (Fig. S3A–C), implying excellent selectivity of T-CDs@Al3+ for PPi. What’s more, other competitive substances with high concentrations (up to 160 μM) don’t display obviously interference (Fig. S3A–C).

We investigated PPi sensing ability of T-CDs@Al3+ (22.5 μM Al3+) in the scope of 0–400 μM under pH 7.2 condition at 420 nm excitation. When PPi concentrations increase, FL intensities of T-CDs@Al3+ at both 570 and 606 nm enhance while that at 473 nm decreases (Fig. 5 A). Fig. 5B presents the relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and PPi concentrations by an exceptional Logistic model, in which the fitting equation is y = 6.4602 − 5.8590/(1+(x/0.9995)^1.3349). An excellent linear relationship with R2 = 0.9984 is displayed in the scope of 0.75–10 μM and the LOD of 2.2 nM is gained on basis of the signal/noise ratio of 3. Compared with previous reports in Table S2, the proposed PPi probe displays superior sensitivity.

Absorbance titrations were operated to investigate the PPi sensing capability of T-CDs@Al3+ system. With rising PPi concentrations, the absorbance intensity at 540 nm increases by degrees accompanying with a ratio decrease at 278 nm (Fig. 5C). The relationship between absorbance intensity ratios (A 278/A 540) and PPi concentrations shows an exceptional Logistic model with the fitting equation of y = 1.3195 + 0.3360/(1+(x/29.5367)^2.1251) in Fig. 5D. An excellent linear relationship with R2 = 0.9943 is gained in the scope of 0–50 μM and the LOD is calculated as 0.5 μM. As shown in inset of Fig. 5C, upon introducing PPi to T-CDs@Al3+ solution, Al3+ can be competitively captured by PPi, causing the disappearance of the shoulder peak at 305 nm, the decrease of absorption intensity at 278 nm, and the increase of absorption intensity at 540 nm.

We studied the colorimetric sensing capability of T-CDs@Al3+ via surveying color change on introduction of PPi under daylight. Adding PPi to T-CDs@Al3+ solution leads to a remarkable colour change from pink to orange (Fig. 5E), resulting from the departure of Al3+ from T-CDs’ surface. Therefore, colorimetric sensing of PPi is of quite feasibility. These results present that T-CDs@Al3+ may be applied for the triple-signal sensor to discriminate PPi.

Al3+ with more positive charges and smaller radius is thought as hard acid. T-CDs’ surface is covered with carboxyl groups. O of carboxyl groups is regarded as hard base. As Al3+ is added to T-CDs solution, O of carboxyl groups may combine strongly with Al3+ on basis of the rule of hard and soft acids and bases. To support the proposed binding of T-CDs and Al3+, zeta potentials were measured and FTIR spectra were taken. Zeta potential of T-CDs solution increases remarkably from −27.3 to +1.8 mV (Fig. S4) upon adding Al3+, probably attributing to carboxyl anions’ expenditure and confirming the successful combination of Al3+ with T-CDs. Furthermore, compared with FTIR spectrum of T-CDs, the appearance of three peaks at 1518.83, 1231.09 and 1113.47 cm–1 in FTIR spectrum of T-CDs@Al3+ confirms the formation of AlO bond (Fig. S1). Additionally, as shown in TEM (Fig. S5), average diameter of T-CDs varies from 3.18 to 7.51 nm upon adding Al3+, revealing that the combination of Al3+ with T-CDs induces the agglomeration of T-CDs (Fig. 6 A). On one hand, the combination of Al3+ with T-CDs makes energy transfer of T-CDs’ surface occur, weakening FL intensity of yellow and orange emission peaks. On the other hand, the agglomeration of T-CDs enlarges π-conjugated system of carbon core, enhancing FL intensity of the blue emission peak.

Upon adding PPi, Al3+ can be taken away from T-CDs’ surface based on the chelating effect of PPi on Al3+. To verify the proposed interaction, zeta potentials were measured. PPi makes zeta potential of T-CDs@Al3+ change from +1.8 to –10.9 mV (Fig. S4), attributing to the separation of Al3+ from T-CDs’ surface and confirming the chelation between PPi and Al3+. Three PPi provide six coordinated oxygen atoms to Al3+, forming three six-membered rings around Al3+ (Fig. 6B). Furthermore, as illustrated in TEM (Fig. S5), PPi makes average diameter of T-CDs decrease from 7.51 to 3.24 nm, revealing that the aggregation of T-CDs releases. Thus, subsequent addition of PPi to T-CDs@Al3+ solution not only forbids energy transfer of T-CDs’ surface, enhancing FL intensity of yellow and orange emission peaks, but also weakens π-conjugated system of carbon core, decreasing FL intensity of the blue emission peak.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Al3+ and PPi detection in tap water and human blood serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: To test the practicability of the developed method, standard recovery experiments were performed in tap water and human blood serum samples. The recoveries of Al3+ and PPi in tap water were found to be 97.1–103.4 % and 96.71–105.2 % (Table S3), respectively. The recoveries of Al3+ and PPi in human blood serum sample were 96.89–104.38 % and 95.88–103.83 % (Table S4), respectively. These results indicate that the proposed method could be extended to practical detections of Al3+ and PPi in real samples.

We evaluated the cytotoxicity of T-CDs via MTT assay in HeLa cells with T-CDs concentrations from 1 to 5 g L−1 so that we could apply T-CDs in living cells. It is demonstrated that cell survival rate is more than 86.7 % (Fig. S6), implying low cell cytotoxicity of T-CDs under the experimental concentration of 1.7 g L−1. T-CDs entered the cells possibly through endocytosis [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Al3+ and PPi in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Laser scanning confocal microscopy can monitor and analyze three-dimensional structure of cells in real time with high temporal and spatial resolution, which is of extraordinary significance for intracellular detection of analytes and early diagnosis of human diseases. Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells were displayed in Fig. 7 A–C. T-CDs-stained cells show strong yellow and orange emissions but weak blue one (Fig. 7A). Upon introducing Al3+, yellow and orange emissions fade while the blue emission glows (Fig. 7B). On subsequent adding PPi, blue emission fades while yellow and orange ones glow (Fig. 7C). Furthermore, the intracellular FL intensities of T-CDs, T-CDs@Al3+ and T-CDs@Al3++PPi are shown in Fig. 7D. And Fig. 7E displays the relationship between FL intensity ratios (I 473/I 570) and T-CDs, T-CDs@Al3+ and T-CDs@Al3++PPi, respectively. These results illustrate that T-CDs could be used as ratiometric nanosensor for continuously detecting Al3+ and PPi in living cells.

Tricolor emission fluorescent T-CDs have been developed through hydrothermal means utilizing hematoxylin and Tris as raw materials. The obtained T-CDs can be designed as a multi-functional sensing platform for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric sensing of Al3+ and PPi in aqueous solution. Furthermore, as-fabricated T-CDs have been extended to discriminate Al3+ and PPi in living cells. It is anticipated that T-CDs have great application prospect in disease diagnosis and biological sensing.

Lihong Shi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Qianliang Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Caihong Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Guomei Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Investigation, Validation. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21874087) and Shanxi Province Hundred Talent Project.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130375.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable photoluminescence of water-soluble AgInZnS–graphene oxide (GO) nanocomposites and their application in-vivo bioimaging
Sens. Actuators B: Chem.

Mater. Chem. Front.

Nanoscale

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Lihong Shi is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2008. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.
Qianliang Wang is now a postgraduate in Shanxi University and major in physical chemistry.
Caihong Zhang is now working as a professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in 2009. Her current research interests include nanomaterial and chemosensors.

Guomei Zhang received her BSc degree from the Shanxi University of China (Taiyuan) in 1997, her MSc degree and Ph.D. degree in analytical and inorganic chemistry in 2003 and 2006 from Shanxi University, respectively. She joined the Chemistry Faculty of Shanxi University in 2006, and master tutor at Shanxi University since 2008 and has been a professor in 2014. Her current research interests include the DNA assembly and photoluminescence analysis of nanomaterials.

Yan Zhang received is now working as an associate professor in Shanxi University, Shanxi Province, China. She obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Shanxi University in 2008. Her current research interests include metal nanomaterials.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He obtained his Ph.D. degree at Shanxi University in 2002. Dong has published over 200 papers and 100 patents and his current research interests are biosensors, chemosensors, optical fiber sensor, fluorescence and room temperature phosphorescence, and environmental analysis and monitoring.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. She has published extensively in journals such as Nanoscale, Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Chemical Communications, Sensors and Actuators B, Analytical Chemistry, Talanta etc. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemsitry, and photochemsity.
